name: Kelpie
description: The Kelpie is a shape-shifting water spirit inhabiting the lochs and pools of Scotland. It most commonly appears as a beautiful black horse, often with a dripping mane, and lures unsuspecting travelers to their doom. These malevolent creatures are said to drown their victims, tearing them apart, and consuming all but the heart and liver. While usually equine, Kelpies can also take on human form, often as a strikingly attractive man or woman, though they are always betrayed by their dripping hair, or the presence of water weeds in their hair. Some tales suggest they can also manifest as monstrous, scaled humanoids. They are powerful and dangerous beings, deeply connected to the waters they inhabit. Legend dictates that Kelpies can be overcome, but only by seizing their bridle, which grants control over the beast and its immense strength. However, once the bridle is removed, the Kelpie's true nature is unleashed, and it will seek vengeance.
subtitle: Deadly shape-shifting water horse
appearance: Typically appears as a beautiful, powerful black horse with a wet, dripping mane and hooves that are sometimes reversed. Can also assume the form of an attractive human, usually with tell-tale wet hair or water weeds.
habitat: Lochs, rivers, and deep pools throughout Scotland.
behavior: Lures travelers, especially children, to their watery graves. Preys on the unsuspecting, drowning and consuming them. Can be controlled with its bridle, but is inherently malevolent and vengeful.

keyMyths.title: The Kelpie of Loch Ness
keyMyths.description: Local legends tell of a Kelpie residing in Loch Ness, preying on those who venture too close to the water's edge, further fueling the mystery surrounding the Loch Ness Monster. It lures victims in with its beauty before dragging them down into the depths.

sources.author: Walter Scott
sources.description: A collection of Scottish ballads and folklore, including mentions of water spirits akin to Kelpies.
sources.text: A collection of Scottish ballads and folklore, including mentions of water spirits akin to Kelpies.

sources.author: Katharine Briggs
sources.description: A comprehensive guide to fairies and other supernatural beings in British folklore, including detailed descriptions of Kelpies.
sources.text: A comprehensive guide to fairies and other supernatural beings in British folklore, including detailed descriptions of Kelpies.
